prompt: A colleague writes: `for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { total = total + arr[i]; }`. Review this line in a friendly but honest way.
response:
Friendly-but-honest code-review in one breath:

“Hey, your loop works, but idiomatic JS today favours `for (const value of arr)` for readability and because it removes the manual index juggling.  
If you really need the index, keep the `let i` but cache `arr.length` in a local (`const len = arr.length`) so the engine doesn’t look it up every pass; both tweaks drop a couple of cycles and lower the ‘foot-gun’ surface (e.g. someone later rewriting `i++` as `i += 2` or forgetting `let`).  
Nothing world-breaking—just small polish to make the next reader smile.”
